title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an urban beekeeper called David Thorne talking about keeping bees in the city. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an urban beekeeper called David Thorne talking about keeping bees in the city. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: David Thorne explains the benefits and difficulties of managing beehives on city rooftops.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m David Thorne. People are often surprised when I tell them I’m a beekeeper, because I live in the middle of London. But urban beekeeping is a growing trend. [7]I started my first colony after attending a weekend workshop at a community garden[/7]. I was hooked immediately. One of the biggest misconceptions is that city honey is less pure than country honey. In fact, [8]the absence of agricultural pesticides in the city means the honey is often higher quality[/8]. Our bees forage on a massive variety of garden flowers and trees. Maintaining hives on rooftops is quite different from a traditional farm. [9]I found that the most taxing part of the job was carrying heavy equipment up several flights of stairs[/9]. Most of the buildings we use don't have elevators to the roof. We also have to be careful about the wind. [10]We installed a series of wooden windbreaks to protect the hives during winter storms[/10]. It’s essential for the bees' survival. Regarding the community, the response has been amazing. [11]The unexpected benefit of the project was the way it improved the local residents' mental health[/11]. People love coming up to the roof just to watch the bees work. It’s very calming. During the harvest, we involve local schools. [12]Seeing the children's fascination during the honey extraction process was incredibly rewarding[/12]. It’s a great way to teach them about biology. Looking ahead, I want to expand the project. [13]I believe that increasing urban biodiversity is the most effective way to save our bee populations[/13]. We need more green spaces in our cities. As I often say to newcomers, [14]we are not just producing honey, we are 'building ecosystems' in the concrete jungle[/14]. It’s a small step, but a vital one.

dialogue.speaker: david
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m David Thorne. People are often surprised when I tell them I’m a beekeeper, because I live in the middle of London. But urban beekeeping is a growing trend. I started my first colony after attending a weekend workshop at a community garden. I was hooked immediately. One of the biggest misconceptions is that city honey is less pure than country honey. In fact, the absence of agricultural pesticides in the city means the honey is often higher quality. Our bees forage on a massive variety of garden flowers and trees. Maintaining hives on rooftops is quite different from a traditional farm. I found that the most taxing part of the job was carrying heavy equipment up several flights of stairs. Most of the buildings we use don't have elevators to the roof. We also have to be careful about the wind. We installed a series of wooden windbreaks to protect the hives during winter storms. It’s essential for the bees' survival. Regarding the community, the response has been amazing. The unexpected benefit of the project was the way it improved the local residents' mental health. People love coming up to the roof just to watch the bees work. It’s very calming. During the harvest, we involve local schools. Seeing the children's fascination during the honey extraction process was incredibly rewarding. It’s a great way to teach them about biology. Looking ahead, I want to expand the project. I believe that increasing urban biodiversity is the most effective way to save our bee populations. We need more green spaces in our cities. As I often say to newcomers, we are not just producing honey, we are 'building ecosystems' in the concrete jungle. It’s a small step, but a vital one.

questions.explanation: He mentions attending a weekend workshop at a community garden.

questions.explanation: He says the absence of agricultural pesticides means higher quality honey.

questions.question: David says that the most physically demanding part of his work is moving (9) to the rooftops.
questions.explanation: He states carrying heavy equipment up stairs was the most taxing part.

questions.question: The team used (10) as a way to keep the bees safe from bad weather.
questions.explanation: They installed wooden windbreaks to protect the hives.

questions.question: David was surprised by how much beekeeping helped with the (11) of the people living nearby.
questions.explanation: He notes the project improved residents' mental health.

questions.question: David found the stage of (12) to be a particularly good teaching opportunity.
questions.explanation: The fascination during the honey extraction process was rewarding.

questions.question: David is convinced that the best way to help bees is to focus on (13) in cities.
questions.explanation: He believes increasing urban biodiversity is the most effective way.

questions.question: David uses the phrase (14) to describe the larger goal of his beekeeping project.
questions.explanation: He says they are 'building ecosystems' in the concrete jungle.
